head_coach.name: Jesse Minter
head_coach.scheme_philosophy: Modular, multiple defensive system rooted in the Wink Martindale/Mike Macdonald Ravens tree. Emphasizes disguise, late rotations, zone-heavy coverage, light personnel packages (nickel/dime), hybrid safety usage near the box, and four-man pressure with low blitz rates. Prioritizes pass defense and baiting opponents into the run.

defensive_coordinator.name: Anthony Weaver
defensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Baltimore Ravens defensive line tradition; Rex Ryan / Romeo Crennel fronts; worked under Mike Macdonald in Baltimore 2021-2023

offense.scheme_family: Sean Payton / Ben Johnson hybrid — marries Payton's West Coast concepts and schematic multiplicity with Johnson's Shanahan-influenced run-pass marry concepts, motion, and explosive play-hunting
offense.base_personnel: 12 (heavy 12/21/22 personnel under Monken; Doyle likely to increase 11 personnel usage given his background in Payton's spread formations, but Ravens' elite TE room — Andrews, Likely, Kolar — will keep 12 personnel prominent)
offense.run_scheme: Inside zone and outside zone primary, with gap/power mixed in. Ravens have been the NFL's #1 rushing offense in 2 of 3 Monken years. Doyle inherits Derrick Henry and Lamar Jackson as a devastating run tandem. RPO usage was elite under Monken (1st in NFL in RPO calls in 2023).
offense.pass_concepts: Play action heavy off dominant run game, RPO integration, vertical shots off play action, quick game and screens. Doyle's stated priorities: physicality, detail, and explosiveness. Expect emphasis on deception, motion, and big-play hunting consistent with Payton and Johnson influences.
offense.te_usage: Elite multi-TE usage. Ravens ran 12 personnel at 35.9% in 2025, among the highest in the NFL. Mark Andrews and Isaiah Likely are both flexed into receiving roles and used in-line. Andrews is the franchise receiving TE; Likely is a dynamic move piece who functions like a WR from slot/flexed alignments. Doyle coached TEs in Denver under Payton where TEs were heavily involved in passing, rushing, and protection.
offense.rb_usage: Bell cow with Derrick Henry; Justice Hill as change-of-pace/receiving back. Henry is a pure power runner who also catches passes occasionally. Hill posted 42 receptions in 2024 as a strong third-down back. Committee likely to involve Keaton Mitchell's speed.

defense.alignment: Nominally odd front (3-4) but extremely multiple. Minter uses 4-down fronts, 3-down fronts, load fronts, and even zero-DL looks with 3 edge rushers and 6 DBs. Under Minter in LA, Chargers used base defense only ~17% of the time; primary sub-packages are nickel and dime. Minter's scheme is modular — fronts, pressures, and coverages are independent modules combined dynamically.
defense.edge_rush_source: Stand-up OLB/EDGE hybrid. Ravens have historically used stand-up OLBs as primary pass rushers (Oweh, Van Noy). Minter also deploys creative simulated pressures and stunts (9.5% sim pressure rate, 19.8% stunt rate with Chargers). Four-man rush is preferred — Chargers blitzed at only 21.4% under Minter, 3rd fewest in NFL.
defense.coverage_base: Zone heavy, highly multiple. Chargers under Minter in 2025: Cover 3 (31.1%), Cover 2 (25%), Cover 1 (13.1%), Cover 4 (11.7%). Zone coverage on 80.7% of snaps (5th highest in NFL). Split safety looks used 49.6% of time (4th most). Late pre-snap rotations and disguise are a hallmark — designed to stress QB processing.
defense.blitz_tendency: light (21.4% in LA, prefers four-man rush with elite pass rush efficiency; will use simulated pressures and safety blitzes as tendency-breakers)
defense.lb_role: ILBs (Roquan Smith) are coverage and run-stop oriented. OLBs are primary edge rushers. Minter's nickel/dime-heavy approach often reduces ILBs on the field, replacing them with safeties/DBs. ILBs must be able to cover.
defense.safety_usage: Extreme hybrid usage — the defining feature of this defense. Kyle Hamilton is used as a box safety, slot defender, quasi-LB, and occasional edge blitzer. Minter used Derwin James near the box on ~85% of snaps in LA. Hamilton operates similarly in Baltimore. Malaki Starks plays free safety/deep role, allowing Hamilton to roam. Three-safety packages (Hamilton, Starks, Gilman) are a foundational grouping, not a gimmick.

te_receiving_boost.explanation: The Ravens have the NFL's best TE room (Andrews, Likely, Kolar) and have run 12 personnel at one of the highest rates in the league (35.9% in 2025). Both Andrews and Likely function as receiving weapons — Likely especially operates like a WR from flexed/slot alignments. Doyle coached TEs in Denver under Payton where TEs were heavily schemed into the passing game. Any TE with elite receiving traits is valued at near-WR levels in this offense because the scheme creates mismatches by deploying TEs against LBs and safeties in space.

lb_pass_rush_boost.explanation: Baltimore's base is a 3-4 where OLBs are the primary edge rushers, which nominally creates high LB-to-EDGE value. However, Minter's scheme is extremely multiple and often removes traditional LBs from the field entirely in favor of extra DBs. The OLB/EDGE position is critical, but the ILB role is more coverage-oriented than rush-oriented. An LB with pass rush traits fits as a stand-up OLB/EDGE, but the ILB room (Roquan Smith) is asked to cover, not rush. A tweener LB/EDGE with pass rush upside fits the OLB spot very well. A pure inside LB who can also rush the passer has moderate value since Minter occasionally uses simulated pressures from the ILB spot.

rb_dual_threat_boost.explanation: The Ravens' run game is built around a power bell cow (Derrick Henry) and Lamar Jackson's designed runs. The primary RB role is as a runner. However, Justice Hill's 42-reception 2024 season shows the offense does value a receiving back, especially on third downs. Doyle's Payton/Johnson influences tend to feature backs in the passing game. With Henry aging, a dual-threat back who can catch has moderate-to-rising value, but the primary RB is still valued first as a runner in this run-dominant scheme.

s_hybrid_boost.explanation: This is the single most important positional value inflection for Baltimore. Minter's entire defensive system is built around a hybrid safety who can play near the box, in the slot, as a quasi-LB, and as an occasional blitzer — while also covering deep. Kyle Hamilton is the prototype. Minter used Derwin James near the box on ~85% of snaps. The Ravens deploy three-safety packages as a base sub-grouping (Hamilton/Starks/Gilman). Any safety prospect with elite versatility — box tackling, man coverage, blitz ability, and range — gets a massive value boost for Baltimore. Minter specifically said of James: 'The closer he is to the action, the better he is.' He will deploy Hamilton the same way.

scheme_specific_draft_notes: Baltimore's defense under Minter is the NFL's premier destination for hybrid safeties. Any safety with LB-like physicality and DB-level coverage skills (think Derwin James / Kyle Hamilton mold) should be valued significantly higher for BAL than their draft board position suggests. The Ravens already have Hamilton and Starks but actively seek safety depth given their 3-safety base sub-package. On EDGE, Minter prefers versatile pass rushers who can stand up or put their hand in the dirt and also drop into coverage on simulated pressures — pure speed rushers who can bend and stunt are prized. At TE, Baltimore's 12-personnel-heavy scheme with Doyle's Payton-influenced TE usage creates near-WR value for elite receiving TEs. Mark Andrews' age (31) and Isaiah Likely's pending free agency could create draft need at TE. For ILB, Minter's dime-heavy approach reduces traditional LB snaps — only coverage-capable LBs have significant value. At RB, the bell-cow role remains run-first, but Doyle's Payton/Johnson background may increase passing game involvement for backs with receiving chops. On offense, the addition of senior offensive assistant Joe Lombardi (former Chargers/Broncos OC, Payton tree) reinforces the West Coast/Erhardt-Perkins influence that prizes route-running TEs and versatile skill players over pure size.
